result.markdown:
# Final Project Studio ::: {.hero} **Part VII. Responsible AI, Projects, and Deployment** **Chapter goal:** design, execute, and communicate a rigorous final project in modern time series analysis and sequence learning. The project should connect mathematical assumptions, an information-set formulation, leakage-safe validation, interpretable diagnostics, uncertainty, reproducible Python code, interactive visualization, and AI-assisted model criticism. ::: ## Learning objectives After studying this chapter, you should be able to: 1. translate an applied time-series or sequence-data question into a precise mathematical problem; 2. write a project contract specifying target, forecast origin, horizon, information set, loss, baselines, and deliverables; 3. select an appropriate method family from classical statistics, spectral analysis, feature-based ML, deep learning, or representation learning; 4. design a validation scheme that respects time ordering and prevents leakage; 5. evaluate point forecasts, probabilistic forecasts, classifications, anomaly scores, or learned representations; 6. build an interactive HTML component that helps explain the project idea; 7. use AI assistance for critique, code review, and communication while verifying all claims; 8. prepare a reproducible notebook and a clear written report. ## 27.1 What counts as a strong final project? A strong final project is not just a competition to obtain the smallest error. It is a complete mathematical and computational argument. The report should answer four questions: 1. **What is the prediction or inference problem?** 2. **What information is available when the decision is made?** 3. **What model assumptions are being made?** 4. **What evidence supports or contradicts those assumptions?** The project may focus on forecasting, anomaly detection, sequence classification, regime detection, multivariate modeling, spectral analysis, representation learning, or an AI-assisted forecasting workflow. In every case, the project should be organized around an ordered data set $$ \bigl\{(t_i, y_i, x_i): i=1,\ldots,n\bigr\}, $$ where $t_i$ is the time or sequence index, $y_i$ is the target or response, and $x_i$ is an optional vector of covariates. A useful high-level template is $$ \text{scientific question} \longrightarrow \text{statistical task} \longrightarrow \text{information set} \longrightarrow \text{model class} \longrightarrow \text{validation design} \longrightarrow \text{diagnostic evidence}. $$ ::: {.callout-tip} ## Project principle A final project should make it easy for another student to reproduce the result and understand why the validation design is valid for ordered data. ::: ## 27.2 The project contract Before fitting any model, write a **project contract**. This is the mathematical specification of the problem. For forecasting, define the forecast target as $$ Y_{t+h}, \qquad h\in \mathcal H, $$ where $t$ is the forecast origin and $\mathcal H$ is the set of horizons. The information available at the origin is the filtration or information set $$ \mathcal F_t = \sigma\bigl(Y_s, X_s, C_u: s\le t,\ u\le t+h\text{ and known at time }t\bigr), $$ where $C_u$ may include calendar variables known in advance. A valid forecast must have the form $$ \widehat Y_{t+h|t}=g_h(\mathcal F_t). $$ If a variable is not known at time $t$, it must not be used as a feature for predicting $Y_{t+h}$ unless a separate model forecasts that variable first. For sequence classification, define a sequence window $$ \mathbf X_i=(X_{i,1},\ldots,X_{i,L_i}) $$ and a label $Z_i\in\{1,\ldots,K\}$. A classifier has the form $$ \widehat p_\theta(k\mid \mathbf X_i) = P_\theta(Z_i=k\mid \mathbf X_i). $$ For anomaly detection, define a score $$ A_t=s(Y_t,Y_{t-1},\ldots,X_t,X_{t-1},\ldots), $$ where larger values indicate stronger evidence against normal behavior. A threshold rule has the form $$ \widehat Z_t=\mathbf 1\{A_t>c\}. $$ For representation learning, define an encoder $$ z_t = e_\theta(W_t), $$ where $W_t=(Y_{t-L+1},\ldots,Y_t)$ is a time window. The representation should be evaluated through a downstream task such as clustering, classification, anomaly detection, or forecasting. ## 27.3 Choosing the project type The project type should match the structure of the data and the available time. | Project type | Good data situation | Main output | Typical methods | |---|---|---|---| | Forecasting | one or many ordered series | $\widehat Y_{t+h|t}$ | naive, ARIMA, SARIMA, Prophet, ML, neural nets | | Probabilistic forecasting | decision requires uncertainty | intervals or quantiles | bootstrap, conformal, Bayesian, quantile regression | | Anomaly detection | rare unusual events | anomaly score $A_t$ | residual methods, autoencoders, isolation forests | | Sequence classification | each sequence has a label | class probability | feature extraction, CNN, RNN, transformer | | Regime detection | hidden states or structural breaks | inferred state $S_t$ | HMM, state-space, change-point methods | | Spectral analysis | oscillations are central | dominant frequencies | periodogram, smoothing, wavelets | | Representation learning | many windows or many series | embeddings $z_t$ | PCA, autoencoder, contrastive learning | | Multivariate forecasting | many interacting variables | vector forecast | VAR, dynamic regression, global ML | A good project is usually narrow enough to finish but rich enough to demonstrate both theory and computation. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Avoid a project that is too broad "Forecast the stock market using deep learning" is too broad. A better project is: "Compare a naive baseline, ARIMA, and a lag-feature gradient-boosting model for one-day-ahead realized volatility forecasting on a fixed set of assets, evaluated by rolling-origin validation." ::: ## 27.4 Interactive module: project design matrix Use this module to think about project scope. The plot is not a grade. It is a planning tool. A project with high data complexity and high model ambition requires more careful validation and stronger reproducibility.

Interactive Module: Project Design Matrix

Adjust the design choices and inspect the resulting feasibility profile.

## 27.5 Validation design For forecasting, validation should mimic deployment. Suppose $\mathcal T$ is the set of forecast origins and $\mathcal H$ is the set of horizons. For a loss function $\ell$, the rolling-origin validation error is $$ \widehat R = \frac{1}{|\mathcal T||\mathcal H|} \sum_{\tau\in\mathcal T}\sum_{h\in\mathcal H} \ell\left(Y_{\tau+h},\widehat Y_{\tau+h|\tau}\right). $$ A horizon-specific error is $$ \widehat R_h = \frac{1}{|\mathcal T|} \sum_{\tau\in\mathcal T} \ell\left(Y_{\tau+h},\widehat Y_{\tau+h|\tau}\right). $$ This matters because some models perform well for $h=1$ but poorly for longer horizons. For sequence classification, use a split that respects grouping and chronology. If windows overlap heavily, random window splitting can leak nearly identical examples into training and test sets. If a sequence belongs to a patient, asset, device, or user, the split should respect that grouping. For anomaly detection, avoid setting the threshold using future anomalies unless that would also happen in deployment. If labeled anomalies are scarce, use a validation period that is separated from the final test period. ## 27.6 Interactive module: rolling-origin planner

Interactive Module: Rolling-Origin Validation Planner

Visualize how many backtesting forecasts your project will require.

## 27.7 Baselines and model comparison Every project should include at least one baseline. Baselines are not optional. They prevent students from mistaking complexity for performance. For a univariate forecasting project, common baselines include: $$ \widehat Y_{t+h|t}^{\mathrm{naive}}=Y_t, $$ and for seasonal data with period $s$, $$ \widehat Y_{t+h|t}^{\mathrm{seasonal}}=Y_{t+h-ks}, $$ where $k$ is chosen so that $t+h-ks\le t$. For sequence classification, compare against majority-class prediction, logistic regression on summary features, or a simple CNN before using a large transformer. For anomaly detection, compare against a residual threshold or rolling z-score before using an autoencoder. A recommended project model ladder is: $$ \text{baseline} \rightarrow \text{interpretable statistical model} \rightarrow \text{feature-based ML} \rightarrow \text{deep model, if justified}. $$ The final model does not need to be the most complex model. It should be the model best supported by validation and diagnostics. ## 27.8 Python example: a small final-project prototype The following example simulates daily demand data, creates a chronological split, evaluates naive and seasonal-naive baselines, and fits a small lag-feature regression model. It illustrates the minimum level of computational reproducibility expected in a project. ::: {#cell-py-ch27-project-prototype .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error rng = np.random.default_rng(7339) n = 420 t = np.arange(n) weekly = 8 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 7) annualish = 3 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 90) trend = 0.04 * t noise = rng.normal(0, 2.0, size=n) y = 80 + trend + weekly + annualish + noise df = pd.DataFrame({"t": t, "y": y}) for lag in [1, 7, 14, 28]: df[f"lag_{lag}"] = df["y"].shift(lag) df["sin7"] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df["t"] / 7) df["cos7"] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df["t"] / 7) df = df.dropna().reset_index(drop=True) train_size = 300 train = df.iloc[:train_size].copy() test = df.iloc[train_size:].copy() feature_cols = ["lag_1", "lag_7", "lag_14", "lag_28", "sin7", "cos7"] model = Ridge(alpha=1.0) model.fit(train[feature_cols], train["y"]) test = test.assign( naive=test["lag_1"], seasonal_naive=test["lag_7"], ridge=model.predict(test[feature_cols]) ) summary = [] for name in ["naive", "seasonal_naive", "ridge"]: mae = mean_absolute_error(test["y"], test[name]) rmse = mean_squared_error(test["y"], test[name]) ** 0.5 summary.append({"model": name, "MAE": mae, "RMSE": rmse}) summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary) print(summary_df.round(3)) plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4)) plt.plot(train["t"], train["y"], label="training data") plt.plot(test["t"], test["y"], label="test data") plt.plot(test["t"], test["seasonal_naive"], label="seasonal naive") plt.plot(test["t"], test["ridge"], label="ridge forecast") plt.axvline(train["t"].iloc[-1], linestyle="--", linewidth=1) plt.xlabel("time") plt.ylabel("demand") plt.title("Prototype forecast comparison") plt.legend() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` model MAE RMSE 0 naive 4.844 5.517 1 seasonal_naive 2.251 2.799 2 ridge 1.846 2.353 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Prototype final-project workflow with baselines and a lag-feature regression model.](chapter-27-final-project-studio_files/figure-epub/py-ch27-project-prototype-output-2.png){#py-ch27-project-prototype} ::: ::: The example is intentionally modest. A final project should extend this idea by adding rolling-origin validation, residual diagnostics, uncertainty quantification, and a discussion of assumptions. ## 27.9 Python example: rolling-origin evaluation The next example evaluates a one-step-ahead ridge model over many forecast origins. The important point is that the model is refit at each origin using only past information. ::: {#cell-py-ch27-rolling-origin .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} from sklearn.base import clone origins = np.arange(220, len(df) - 1, 7) records = [] for origin in origins: train_o = df.iloc[:origin].copy() test_o = df.iloc[[origin]].copy() mdl = clone(model) mdl.fit(train_o[feature_cols], train_o["y"]) pred_ridge = float(mdl.predict(test_o[feature_cols])[0]) pred_naive = float(test_o["lag_1"].iloc[0]) pred_seasonal = float(test_o["lag_7"].iloc[0]) actual = float(test_o["y"].iloc[0]) records.extend([ {"origin": int(origin), "model": "ridge", "error": actual - pred_ridge}, {"origin": int(origin), "model": "naive", "error": actual - pred_naive}, {"origin": int(origin), "model": "seasonal_naive", "error": actual - pred_seasonal}, ]) err_df = pd.DataFrame(records) roll_summary = ( err_df.assign(abs_error=lambda d: d["error"].abs()) .groupby("model", as_index=False) .agg(MAE=("abs_error", "mean"), Bias=("error", "mean")) ) print(roll_summary.round(3)) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4)) for name, g in err_df.groupby("model"): plt.plot(g["origin"], g["error"], marker="o", linewidth=1, label=name) plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1) plt.xlabel("forecast origin") plt.ylabel("forecast error") plt.title("Rolling-origin forecast errors") plt.legend() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` model MAE Bias 0 naive 4.789 -4.789 1 ridge 1.679 0.385 2 seasonal_naive 1.738 0.256 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Rolling-origin validation errors for a lag-feature forecasting model.](chapter-27-final-project-studio_files/figure-epub/py-ch27-rolling-origin-output-2.png){#py-ch27-rolling-origin} ::: ::: ## 27.10 Diagnostics and evidence The project report should include evidence that the chosen model is adequate for the task. The diagnostic evidence depends on the project type. For forecasting, include: - time plot of actual values and forecasts; - horizon-wise error table; - residual ACF or error autocorrelation; - residual plot over time; - comparison against baselines; - prediction-interval coverage, if intervals are used. For classification, include: - train/validation/test split description; - confusion matrix; - precision, recall, F1, and calibration if probabilities are used; - an explanation of how overlapping windows are handled; - a baseline classifier. For anomaly detection, include: - anomaly-score time plot; - threshold selection rule; - false-positive/false-negative discussion if labels exist; - examples of detected anomalies; - sensitivity analysis for the threshold. For spectral analysis, include: - time plot; - periodogram or smoothed spectral estimate; - discussion of aliasing and sampling frequency; - comparison against time-domain evidence; - uncertainty or robustness checks. ## 27.11 Interactive module: project rubric dashboard This dashboard translates the final-project expectations into a self-check score. It is designed for project planning, not for automatic grading.

Interactive Module: Final Project Rubric Dashboard

Move the sliders to estimate the current state of your project draft.

## 27.12 AI-assisted project workflow AI tools can help students plan, code, debug, and communicate. They should not replace statistical reasoning. In this course, AI assistance should be used as a **critic** and **co-pilot**, not as an authority. A useful AI-assisted workflow is: 1. Write the project contract yourself. 2. Ask AI to identify possible leakage and invalid validation. 3. Ask AI to propose baseline methods. 4. Ask AI to review code for time-index mistakes. 5. Ask AI to critique residual diagnostics. 6. Ask AI to rewrite the report for clarity. 7. Verify every technical claim independently. ::: {.callout-ai} ## AI-assisted study box Use this prompt after you have a first draft: > I am writing a final project for a graduate course on modern time series analysis and sequence learning. My task is [forecasting / anomaly detection / classification / representation learning]. My target is [...]. My information set at the decision time is [...]. My validation design is [...]. My baselines are [...]. My main model is [...]. Please act as a skeptical reviewer. Identify possible leakage, weak assumptions, missing diagnostics, unsupported conclusions, and unclear mathematical definitions. For each issue, suggest one concrete verification step. ::: ## 27.13 Required final-project deliverables Each final project should include the following deliverables. ### Written report The written report should contain: 1. title and abstract; 2. data description; 3. project contract; 4. mathematical formulation; 5. baseline methods; 6. main model or models; 7. validation design; 8. results and diagnostics; 9. uncertainty or limitations; 10. AI-assisted workflow note; 11. conclusion. ### Reproducible notebook The notebook should run from top to bottom and produce the main results. It should include: - package imports; - data-loading or simulation section; - preprocessing section; - baseline models; - main model; - validation loop; - figures and tables; - diagnostic checks; - final summary. ### Interactive HTML component The interactive component can be simple. It should teach one important idea from the project, such as: - how a forecast horizon changes the training examples; - how a changepoint changes forecast behavior; - how an anomaly threshold changes false positives; - how an attention map changes across a sequence; - how rolling-origin validation differs from random splitting; - how a frequency peak appears in the periodogram. ### Short presentation The presentation should explain: 1. the problem; 2. the model; 3. the validation design; 4. the main result; 5. one limitation. ## 27.14 Suggested project topics ### Forecasting topics - electricity demand forecasting; - public transportation ridership; - financial volatility; - retail demand; - air quality or temperature forecasting; - web traffic or app usage; - academic enrollment or course demand. ### Sequence-learning topics - sensor sequence classification; - ECG or biomedical signal classification; - anomaly detection in machine monitoring; - activity recognition; - sequence embeddings and clustering; - transformer-based sequence classification. ### Spectral and multiresolution topics - detecting dominant frequencies in climate or signal data; - comparing Fourier and wavelet analysis; - filtering and denoising; - detecting transient bursts; - seasonal-frequency interpretation. ### AI-assisted modeling topics - compare human-designed and AI-suggested forecasting workflows; - audit AI-generated time-series code for leakage; - build an AI-assisted residual-diagnostics checklist; - evaluate whether AI can correctly identify ARIMA/SARIMA structure from plots. ## 27.15 Common mistakes ::: {.callout-warning} ## Common mistakes 1. **Using random train-test splits for forecasting.** This usually leaks future structure. 2. **Skipping baselines.** A complex model that does not beat seasonal naive is not useful. 3. **Scaling on the full data set.** Fit scalers only on training data within each split. 4. **Using future covariates.** Only use variables known at the forecast origin. 5. **Reporting only one split.** A single lucky split can be misleading. 6. **Ignoring horizon-specific errors.** One-step performance may not reflect long-horizon behavior. 7. **Showing a model without diagnostics.** Validation error is not the whole story. 8. **Trusting AI-generated code without inspection.** AI code may accidentally use future observations. ::: ## 27.16 Exercises 1. **Project contract.** Write a project contract for a one-week-ahead demand forecasting problem. Identify $Y_t$, $\mathcal F_t$, $\mathcal H$, the loss function, and two baselines. 2. **Leakage audit.** Give three examples of leakage in a feature-based forecasting project. For each one, write a unit test or diagnostic check. 3. **Rolling-origin design.** Suppose $n=1000$, initial training size is $500$, horizon is $H=20$, and origins are spaced every $10$ observations. How many forecast origins are available? 4. **Baseline comparison.** Simulate a seasonal time series and compare naive, seasonal naive, and a lag-feature linear model. 5. **Interactive design.** Propose one interactive visualization for a final project on anomaly detection. 6. **AI critique.** Ask an AI assistant to critique your project design. Then write a paragraph explaining which suggestions you accepted, rejected, or verified. ## 27.17 Mini project Choose one of the following mini-project prompts and complete a two-page report plus one notebook. ### Option A: Forecasting mini project Use a real or simulated time series. Compare at least three models: naive, seasonal naive, and one learned model. Use rolling-origin validation and report MAE/RMSE. Include one residual diagnostic and one limitation. ### Option B: Anomaly detection mini project Create or find a time series with anomalies. Build at least two anomaly scores. Compare threshold behavior. Include an interactive threshold explorer. ### Option C: Sequence classification mini project Use labeled sequences or simulated sequences. Compare summary-feature logistic regression with a neural sequence model. Use a split that avoids overlap leakage. ### Option D: AI-assisted modeling mini project Generate a time-series modeling plan with an AI assistant. Audit the plan for leakage, missing baselines, invalid diagnostics, and unclear assumptions. Implement a corrected version. ## 27.18 Final checklist Before submitting the final project, check the following. - The problem is mathematically defined. - The information set $\mathcal F_t$ is explicit. - The validation split respects time or sequence grouping. - At least one baseline is included. - The main model is justified by the data and task. - The report includes diagnostic plots or tables. - The notebook runs from top to bottom. - The interactive HTML component works without loading Plotly.js again. - The AI-assisted component is documented and verified. - The conclusion states limitations, not only successes. ::: {.callout-note} ## End of the book This final chapter turns the methods of the course into an independent project. A successful project demonstrates mathematical formulation, computational skill, diagnostic judgment, communication, and responsible use of AI tools. :::
